item.title: Fusing Representation Spaces: A Projected-Fusion Approach to Contrastive Learning
item.dc:creator: Nankai Lin
Kezhou Chen
Haonan Liu
Hongbin Zhang
Shengyi Jiang
Aimin Yang
item.description: The model robustness and generalization in Supervised Learning (SL) has long been a question of great interest. Recent attempts have constructed the target model with Contrastive Learning (CL). Most of the research on CL focuses on Feature-based Contrastive Learning (FeaCL), which uses semantic representations to build the contrastive objective function. In addition, there are also emerging efforts to construct the contrastive objective function from the perspective of label probability distributions. This method is referred to as Probability-based Contrastive Learning (ProCL). However, both FeaCL and ProCL may suffer from a common problem that they can only consider a single type of representation. To this end, we provide a novel framework of CL, named Projected-Fusion Contrastive Learning (PFCL), to combine the advantages of two CL paradigms through representation fusion. Specifically, we first construct a label representation to approximate the label probability distribution outputs. This representation is then fused with the semantic representation by aligning them closely when they share samples with the same labels. Such fusion not only aligns but also enhances the interaction between different representation spaces, thereby promoting comprehensive representational coherence. Experiments prove the effectiveness of our proposed PFCL paradigm. Published: 2026-02-28T16:08:45+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.818 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Nankai Lin; Kezhou Chen; Haonan Liu; Hongbin Zhang; Shengyi Jiang; Aimin Yang

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104265

Relevance: 0.818 (must_read)

The model robustness and generalization in Supervised Learning (SL) has long been a question of great interest. Recent attempts have constructed the target model with Contrastive Learning (CL). Most of the research on CL focuses on Feature-based Contrastive Learning (FeaCL), which uses semantic representations to build the contrastive objective function. In addition, there are also emerging efforts to construct the contrastive objective function from the perspective of label probability distributions. This method is referred to as Probability-based Contrastive Learning (ProCL). However, both FeaCL and ProCL may suffer from a common problem that they can only consider a single type of representation. To this end, we provide a novel framework of CL, named Projected-Fusion Contrastive Learning (PFCL), to combine the advantages of two CL paradigms through representation fusion. Specifically, we first construct a label representation to approximate the label probability distribution outputs. This representation is then fused with the semantic representation by aligning them closely when they share samples with the same labels. Such fusion not only aligns but also enhances the interaction between different representation spaces, thereby promoting comprehensive representational coherence. Experiments prove the effectiveness of our proposed PFCL paradigm.

item.title: Don't let the information slip away
item.dc:creator: Taozhe Li
Guansu Wang
Bo Yu
Yiming Liu
Wei Sun
item.description: Real-time object detection has advanced rapidly in recent years. The YOLO series of detectors is among the most well-known CNN-based object detection models and cannot be overlooked. The latest version, YOLOv26, was recently released, while YOLOv12 achieved state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance with 55.2 mAP on the COCO val2017 dataset. Meanwhile, transformer-based object detection models, also known as DEtection TRansformer (DETR), have demonstrated impressive performance. RT-DETR is an outstanding model that outperformed the YOLO series in both speed and accuracy when it was released. Its successor, RT-DETRv2, achieved 53.4 mAP on the COCO val2017 dataset. However, despite their remarkable performance, all these models let information to slip away. They primarily focus on the features of foreground objects while neglecting the contextual information provided by the background. We believe that background information can significantly aid object detection tasks. For example, cars are more likely to appear on roads rather than in offices, while wild animals are more likely to be found in forests or remote areas rather than on busy streets. To address this gap, we propose an object detection model called Association DETR, which achieves state-of-the-art results compared to other object detection models on the COCO val2017 dataset. Published: 2026-02-26T03:58:41+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.802 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Taozhe Li; Guansu Wang; Bo Yu; Yiming Liu; Wei Sun

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.802 (must_read)

Real-time object detection has advanced rapidly in recent years. The YOLO series of detectors is among the most well-known CNN-based object detection models and cannot be overlooked. The latest version, YOLOv26, was recently released, while YOLOv12 achieved state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance with 55.2 mAP on the COCO val2017 dataset. Meanwhile, transformer-based object detection models, also known as DEtection TRansformer (DETR), have demonstrated impressive performance. RT-DETR is an outstanding model that outperformed the YOLO series in both speed and accuracy when it was released. Its successor, RT-DETRv2, achieved 53.4 mAP on the COCO val2017 dataset. However, despite their remarkable performance, all these models let information to slip away. They primarily focus on the features of foreground objects while neglecting the contextual information provided by the background. We believe that background information can significantly aid object detection tasks. For example, cars are more likely to appear on roads rather than in offices, while wild animals are more likely to be found in forests or remote areas rather than on busy streets. To address this gap, we propose an object detection model called Association DETR, which achieves state-of-the-art results compared to other object detection models on the COCO val2017 dataset.

item.title: SP-UIQA: Fusion Segmentation Priors and Multi-Scale Features for Underwater Image Quality Assessment
item.dc:creator: Jingchun Zhou
Wenbo Guo
Dehuan Zhang
Guangyong Chen
Qiuping Jiang
Ghulam Muhammad
item.description: Underwater image quality assessment (UIQA) is significantly influenced by factors such as color shift, visibility degradation, and structural decay. Existing methods often oversimplify heterogeneous degradations into a single score, which may lead to semantic and spatial mismatches. To address this challenge, we propose SP-UIQA , a Segmentation Priors-guided approach for underwater image quality assessment. SP-UIQA utilizes multi-instance soft masks generated by the Segment Anything Model (SAM) as spatial priors. A Swin Transformer backbone is combined with a mask-guided multi-scale fusion strategy to create spatially-aware degradation representations. We further introduce a multi-dimensional attribute modeling module to decouple color, texture, and structure attributes. Additionally, a spatially-aware gated cross-attention head integrates quality cues to accurately regress the quality score. Experimental results on multiple datasets demonstrate that SP-UIQA achieves superior performance compared to existing methods, showing strong robustness in modeling heterogeneous distortions and preserving key regions in complex underwater scenes. Published: 2026-03-01T15:02:25+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.798 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jingchun Zhou; Wenbo Guo; Dehuan Zhang; Guangyong Chen; Qiuping Jiang; Ghulam Muhammad

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104257

Relevance: 0.798 (must_read)

Underwater image quality assessment (UIQA) is significantly influenced by factors such as color shift, visibility degradation, and structural decay. Existing methods often oversimplify heterogeneous degradations into a single score, which may lead to semantic and spatial mismatches. To address this challenge, we propose SP-UIQA , a Segmentation Priors-guided approach for underwater image quality assessment. SP-UIQA utilizes multi-instance soft masks generated by the Segment Anything Model (SAM) as spatial priors. A Swin Transformer backbone is combined with a mask-guided multi-scale fusion strategy to create spatially-aware degradation representations. We further introduce a multi-dimensional attribute modeling module to decouple color, texture, and structure attributes. Additionally, a spatially-aware gated cross-attention head integrates quality cues to accurately regress the quality score. Experimental results on multiple datasets demonstrate that SP-UIQA achieves superior performance compared to existing methods, showing strong robustness in modeling heterogeneous distortions and preserving key regions in complex underwater scenes.

item.title: Small Object Detection Model with Spatial Laplacian Pyramid Attention and Multi-Scale Features Enhancement in Aerial Images
item.dc:creator: Zhangjian Ji
Huijia Yan
Shaotong Qiao
Kai Feng
Wei Wei
item.description: Detecting objects in aerial images confronts some significant challenges, including small size, dense and non-uniform distribution of objects over high-resolution images, which makes detection inefficient. Thus, in this paper, we proposed a small object detection algorithm based on a Spatial Laplacian Pyramid Attention and Multi-Scale Feature Enhancement in aerial images. Firstly, in order to improve the feature representation of ResNet-50 on small objects, we presented a novel Spatial Laplacian Pyramid Attention (SLPA) module, which is integrated after each stage of ResNet-50 to identify and emphasize important local regions. Secondly, to enhance the model's semantic understanding and features representation, we designed a Multi-Scale Feature Enhancement Module (MSFEM), which is incorporated into the lateral connections of C5 layer for building Feature Pyramid Network (FPN). Finally, the features representation quality of traditional feature pyramid network will be affected because the features are not aligned when the upper and lower layers are fused. In order to handle it, we utilized deformable convolutions to align the features in the fusion processing of the upper and lower levels of the Feature Pyramid Network, which can help enhance the model's ability to detect and recognize small objects. The extensive experimental results on two benchmark datasets: VisDrone and DOTA demonstrate that our improved model performs better for small object detection in aerial images compared to the original algorithm. Published: 2026-02-26T14:11:30+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.791 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhangjian Ji; Huijia Yan; Shaotong Qiao; Kai Feng; Wei Wei

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.791 (must_read)

Detecting objects in aerial images confronts some significant challenges, including small size, dense and non-uniform distribution of objects over high-resolution images, which makes detection inefficient. Thus, in this paper, we proposed a small object detection algorithm based on a Spatial Laplacian Pyramid Attention and Multi-Scale Feature Enhancement in aerial images. Firstly, in order to improve the feature representation of ResNet-50 on small objects, we presented a novel Spatial Laplacian Pyramid Attention (SLPA) module, which is integrated after each stage of ResNet-50 to identify and emphasize important local regions. Secondly, to enhance the model's semantic understanding and features representation, we designed a Multi-Scale Feature Enhancement Module (MSFEM), which is incorporated into the lateral connections of C5 layer for building Feature Pyramid Network (FPN). Finally, the features representation quality of traditional feature pyramid network will be affected because the features are not aligned when the upper and lower layers are fused. In order to handle it, we utilized deformable convolutions to align the features in the fusion processing of the upper and lower levels of the Feature Pyramid Network, which can help enhance the model's ability to detect and recognize small objects. The extensive experimental results on two benchmark datasets: VisDrone and DOTA demonstrate that our improved model performs better for small object detection in aerial images compared to the original algorithm.

item.title: Stable Adaptive Thinking via Advantage Shaping and Length-Aware Gradient Regulation
item.dc:creator: Zihang Xu
Haozhi Xie
Ziqi Miao
Wuxuan Gong
Chen Qian
Lijun Li
item.description: Large reasoning models (LRMs) achieve strong performance through extended reasoning traces, but they often exhibit overthinking behavior for low-complexity queries. Existing efforts to mitigate this issue are fundamentally limited by unstable accuracy-efficiency trade-offs and poor robustness to heterogeneous reasoning behaviors. To address these challenges, we propose a two-stage framework for stable adaptive thinking in LRMs. The framework first applies Hybrid Fine-Tuning to expose the model to both thinking and no-thinking behaviors, establishing well-conditioned initialization. It then performs adaptive reinforcement learning with Correctness-Preserving Advantage Shaping (CPAS) to avoid suppressing correct long-chain reasoning, and Length-Aware Gradient Regulation (LAGR) to stabilize optimization under severe reasoning-length heterogeneity. Extensive experiments on Qwen2.5-1.5B and 7B show consistent improvements over strong baselines, achieving up to +3.7/+3.6 accuracy points while reducing generated tokens by 40.6%/43.9%. Further analyses across varying problem difficulties and out-of-distribution tasks confirm the robustness and generalization of our approach. Published: 2026-02-26T02:49:36+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.791 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zihang Xu; Haozhi Xie; Ziqi Miao; Wuxuan Gong; Chen Qian; Lijun Li

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.791 (must_read)

Large reasoning models (LRMs) achieve strong performance through extended reasoning traces, but they often exhibit overthinking behavior for low-complexity queries. Existing efforts to mitigate this issue are fundamentally limited by unstable accuracy-efficiency trade-offs and poor robustness to heterogeneous reasoning behaviors. To address these challenges, we propose a two-stage framework for stable adaptive thinking in LRMs. The framework first applies Hybrid Fine-Tuning to expose the model to both thinking and no-thinking behaviors, establishing well-conditioned initialization. It then performs adaptive reinforcement learning with Correctness-Preserving Advantage Shaping (CPAS) to avoid suppressing correct long-chain reasoning, and Length-Aware Gradient Regulation (LAGR) to stabilize optimization under severe reasoning-length heterogeneity. Extensive experiments on Qwen2.5-1.5B and 7B show consistent improvements over strong baselines, achieving up to +3.7/+3.6 accuracy points while reducing generated tokens by 40.6%/43.9%. Further analyses across varying problem difficulties and out-of-distribution tasks confirm the robustness and generalization of our approach.

item.title: A Novel Knowledge Distillation Framework to Optimize DETR for Sonar Image Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Shibo Yang
Xiaoyu Zhang
Panlong Tan
Yiwen Liu
item.description: This work presents a new knowledge distillation method referred to as Better Teach Small DETR (BTS-DETR). The method takes into full consideration the characteristics of sonar images and innovatively introduces the memory and object queries as the distillation point. To realize the more efficient transfer of foreground information, we design a foreground and important background token/queries distillation strategy for memory and object query, adaptively adjusting the number of tokens/queries used for distillation. Furthermore, to better optimize the weights of different distillation losses, an adaptive weight allocation distillation loss function is designed, thereby rendering the distillation focus on important regions. BTS-DETR can also be used in conjunction with most existing methods. Compared to the training process of the original detector, BTS-DETR increases negligible computational cost, and the inference process of the trained detector does not induce any additional computational cost. On both the improved UATD dataset and the custom side-scan sonar image dataset, the proposed BTS-DETR consistently improves the performance of Deformable DETR and RT-DETR with smaller backbones across multiple evaluation metrics. Published: 2026-02-28T07:44:03+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.789 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shibo Yang; Xiaoyu Zhang; Panlong Tan; Yiwen Liu

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.115627

Relevance: 0.789 (must_read)

This work presents a new knowledge distillation method referred to as Better Teach Small DETR (BTS-DETR). The method takes into full consideration the characteristics of sonar images and innovatively introduces the memory and object queries as the distillation point. To realize the more efficient transfer of foreground information, we design a foreground and important background token/queries distillation strategy for memory and object query, adaptively adjusting the number of tokens/queries used for distillation. Furthermore, to better optimize the weights of different distillation losses, an adaptive weight allocation distillation loss function is designed, thereby rendering the distillation focus on important regions. BTS-DETR can also be used in conjunction with most existing methods. Compared to the training process of the original detector, BTS-DETR increases negligible computational cost, and the inference process of the trained detector does not induce any additional computational cost. On both the improved UATD dataset and the custom side-scan sonar image dataset, the proposed BTS-DETR consistently improves the performance of Deformable DETR and RT-DETR with smaller backbones across multiple evaluation metrics.

item.title: UFO-DETR: Frequency-Guided End-to-End Detector for UAV Tiny Objects
item.dc:creator: Yuankai Chen
Kai Lin
Qihong Wu
Xinxuan Yang
Jiashuo Lai
Ruoen Chen
Haonan Shi
Minfan He
Meihua Wang
item.description: Small target detection in UAV imagery faces significant challenges such as scale variations, dense distribution, and the dominance of small targets. Existing algorithms rely on manually designed components, and general-purpose detectors are not optimized for UAV images, making it difficult to balance accuracy and complexity. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an end-to-end object detection framework, UFO-DETR, which integrates an LSKNet-based backbone network to optimize the receptive field and reduce the number of parameters. By combining the DAttention and AIFI modules, the model flexibly models multi-scale spatial relationships, improving multi-scale target detection performance. Additionally, the DynFreq-C3 module is proposed to enhance small target detection capability through cross-space frequency feature enhancement. Experimental results show that, compared to RT-DETR-L, the proposed method offers significant advantages in both detection performance and computational efficiency, providing an efficient solution for UAV edge computing. Published: 2026-02-26T07:37:45+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.789 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yuankai Chen; Kai Lin; Qihong Wu; Xinxuan Yang; Jiashuo Lai; Ruoen Chen; Haonan Shi; Minfan He; Meihua Wang

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.789 (must_read)

Small target detection in UAV imagery faces significant challenges such as scale variations, dense distribution, and the dominance of small targets. Existing algorithms rely on manually designed components, and general-purpose detectors are not optimized for UAV images, making it difficult to balance accuracy and complexity. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an end-to-end object detection framework, UFO-DETR, which integrates an LSKNet-based backbone network to optimize the receptive field and reduce the number of parameters. By combining the DAttention and AIFI modules, the model flexibly models multi-scale spatial relationships, improving multi-scale target detection performance. Additionally, the DynFreq-C3 module is proposed to enhance small target detection capability through cross-space frequency feature enhancement. Experimental results show that, compared to RT-DETR-L, the proposed method offers significant advantages in both detection performance and computational efficiency, providing an efficient solution for UAV edge computing.

item.title: Iceberg detection from global open ocean sentinel-1 wave mode SAR data with YOLO deep learning
item.dc:creator: Tao Xie
Chen Wang
Xiao-Ming Li
Huimin Li
Gaojing Fan
Alexis Mouche
Bertrand Chapron
item.description: Sentinel-1 wave mode (WV) synthetic aperture radar (SAR) routinely acquires high-resolution vignettes over the Southern Ocean, but its potential for automated and statistically oriented iceberg study has been underutilized. This work presents IB yolo , a deep learning model adapted from the YOLOv11 architecture, for the detection and localization of km-sized icebergs in open ocean WV SAR data. The model was trained on a manually curated dataset of 13,686 annotated icebergs across 2062 vignettes. Sensitivity experiments over multiple architectural configurations identified the HGNetV2+C2PSA-CAA+P2 combination as optimal with AP 50 : 95 " role="presentation"> 50 : 95 50 : 95 of 0.41. IB yolo generates reliable bounding boxes that enable efficient surface area estimations for icebergs within 0.1-5 km 2 . Application to 19,025 vignettes acquired south of 40 ∘ " role="presentation"> ∘ ∘ S during 2016–2018 yields 80,245 iceberg detections. Most vignettes contain fewer than ten icebergs with total surface areas below 5 km 2 . Icebergs are concentrated near major calving fronts in the northeastern Weddell Sea, Amundsen–Ross Seas, and coastal East Antarctica. Their population shows a pronounced seasonal cycle, with maxima in the austral summer and near-zero values in winter. This aligns with the Altimeter-based analysis for km-sized icebergs, and together they complement global observational records for further understanding of the Antarctic iceberg drift and changes. The retrieved size distribution follows a power-law scaling with an exponent of –1.42, which is consistent with prior satellite-based estimates. These findings confirm the viability of combining WV SAR data with advanced object detection frameworks for scalable iceberg monitoring. The resulting dataset would be a valuable resource for investigating Antarctic iceberg dynamics, freshwater fluxes, and their broader climatic impacts. Published: 2026-02-28T19:58:32+00:00 Venue: Remote Sensing of Environment Score: 0.787 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Tao Xie; Chen Wang; Xiao-Ming Li; Huimin Li; Gaojing Fan; Alexis Mouche; Bertrand Chapron

Journal: Remote Sensing of Environment

DOI: 10.1016/j.rse.2026.115344

Relevance: 0.787 (must_read)

Sentinel-1 wave mode (WV) synthetic aperture radar (SAR) routinely acquires high-resolution vignettes over the Southern Ocean, but its potential for automated and statistically oriented iceberg study has been underutilized. This work presents IB yolo , a deep learning model adapted from the YOLOv11 architecture, for the detection and localization of km-sized icebergs in open ocean WV SAR data. The model was trained on a manually curated dataset of 13,686 annotated icebergs across 2062 vignettes. Sensitivity experiments over multiple architectural configurations identified the HGNetV2+C2PSA-CAA+P2 combination as optimal with AP 50 : 95 " role="presentation"> 50 : 95 50 : 95 of 0.41. IB yolo generates reliable bounding boxes that enable efficient surface area estimations for icebergs within 0.1-5 km 2 . Application to 19,025 vignettes acquired south of 40 ∘ " role="presentation"> ∘ ∘ S during 2016–2018 yields 80,245 iceberg detections. Most vignettes contain fewer than ten icebergs with total surface areas below 5 km 2 . Icebergs are concentrated near major calving fronts in the northeastern Weddell Sea, Amundsen–Ross Seas, and coastal East Antarctica. Their population shows a pronounced seasonal cycle, with maxima in the austral summer and near-zero values in winter. This aligns with the Altimeter-based analysis for km-sized icebergs, and together they complement global observational records for further understanding of the Antarctic iceberg drift and changes. The retrieved size distribution follows a power-law scaling with an exponent of –1.42, which is consistent with prior satellite-based estimates. These findings confirm the viability of combining WV SAR data with advanced object detection frameworks for scalable iceberg monitoring. The resulting dataset would be a valuable resource for investigating Antarctic iceberg dynamics, freshwater fluxes, and their broader climatic impacts.

item.title: RAViT: Resolution-Adaptive Vision Transformer
item.dc:creator: Martial Guidez
Stefan Duffner
Christophe Garcia
item.description: Vision transformers have recently made a breakthrough in computer vision showing excellent performance in terms of precision for numerous applications. However, their computational cost is very high compared to alternative approaches such as Convolutional Neural Networks. To address this problem, we propose a novel framework for image classification called RAViT based on a multi-branch network that operates on several copies of the same image with different resolutions to reduce the computational cost while preserving the overall accuracy. Furthermore, our framework includes an early exit mechanism that makes our model adaptive and allows to choose the appropriate trade-off between accuracy and computational cost at run-time. For example in a two-branch architecture, the original image is first resized to reduce its resolution, then a prediction is performed on it using a first transformer and the resulting prediction is reused together with the original-size image to perform a final prediction on a second transformer with less computation than a classical Vision transformer architecture. The early-exit process allows the model to make a final prediction at intermediate branches, saving even more computation. We evaluated our approach on CIFAR-10, Tiny ImageNet, and ImageNet. We obtained an equivalent accuracy to the classical Vision transformer model with only around 70% of FLOPs. Published: 2026-02-27T16:40:04+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.787 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Martial Guidez; Stefan Duffner; Christophe Garcia

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.787 (must_read)

Vision transformers have recently made a breakthrough in computer vision showing excellent performance in terms of precision for numerous applications. However, their computational cost is very high compared to alternative approaches such as Convolutional Neural Networks. To address this problem, we propose a novel framework for image classification called RAViT based on a multi-branch network that operates on several copies of the same image with different resolutions to reduce the computational cost while preserving the overall accuracy. Furthermore, our framework includes an early exit mechanism that makes our model adaptive and allows to choose the appropriate trade-off between accuracy and computational cost at run-time. For example in a two-branch architecture, the original image is first resized to reduce its resolution, then a prediction is performed on it using a first transformer and the resulting prediction is reused together with the original-size image to perform a final prediction on a second transformer with less computation than a classical Vision transformer architecture. The early-exit process allows the model to make a final prediction at intermediate branches, saving even more computation. We evaluated our approach on CIFAR-10, Tiny ImageNet, and ImageNet. We obtained an equivalent accuracy to the classical Vision transformer model with only around 70% of FLOPs.

item.title: pQuant: Towards Effective Low-Bit Language Models via Decoupled Linear Quantization-Aware Training
item.dc:creator: Wenzheng Zhang
Bingzheng Liu
Yang Hu
Xiaoying Bai
Wentao Zhang
Bin Cui
item.description: Quantization-Aware Training from scratch has emerged as a promising approach for building efficient large language models (LLMs) with extremely low-bit weights (sub 2-bit), which can offer substantial advantages for edge deployment. However, existing methods still fail to achieve satisfactory accuracy and scalability. In this work, we identify a parameter democratization effect as a key bottleneck: the sensitivity of all parameters becomes homogenized, severely limiting expressivity. To address this, we propose pQuant, a method that decouples parameters by splitting linear layers into two specialized branches: a dominant 1-bit branch for efficient computation and a compact high-precision branch dedicated to preserving the most sensitive parameters. Through tailored feature scaling, we explicitly guide the model to allocate sensitive parameters to the high-precision branch. Furthermore, we extend this branch into multiple, sparsely-activated experts, enabling efficient capacity scaling. Extensive experiments indicate our pQuant achieves state-of-the-art performance in extremely low-bit quantization. Published: 2026-02-26T03:51:58+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.786 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Wenzheng Zhang; Bingzheng Liu; Yang Hu; Xiaoying Bai; Wentao Zhang; Bin Cui

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.786 (must_read)

Quantization-Aware Training from scratch has emerged as a promising approach for building efficient large language models (LLMs) with extremely low-bit weights (sub 2-bit), which can offer substantial advantages for edge deployment. However, existing methods still fail to achieve satisfactory accuracy and scalability. In this work, we identify a parameter democratization effect as a key bottleneck: the sensitivity of all parameters becomes homogenized, severely limiting expressivity. To address this, we propose pQuant, a method that decouples parameters by splitting linear layers into two specialized branches: a dominant 1-bit branch for efficient computation and a compact high-precision branch dedicated to preserving the most sensitive parameters. Through tailored feature scaling, we explicitly guide the model to allocate sensitive parameters to the high-precision branch. Furthermore, we extend this branch into multiple, sparsely-activated experts, enabling efficient capacity scaling. Extensive experiments indicate our pQuant achieves state-of-the-art performance in extremely low-bit quantization.

item.title: Feature Space Sharing via Visual Projector for Open-Vocabulary Multi-Label Classification
item.dc:creator: Zhanfang Zhao
Xiaohan Yang
item.description: In real-world image understanding, images often contain multiple semantic concepts, including rare or previously unseen categories, posing significant challenges for multi-label classification. Multi-Label Zero-Shot Learning (ML-ZSL) aims to recognize unseen labels without requiring annotated training data. While recent approaches based on Vision-Language Pre-training (VLP) models have shown promise, they often struggle to preserve fine-grained local visual features that are critical for multi-label recognition. In this work, we present Visual Projector with Feature Space Sharing (VPFSS), a lightweight framework that adapts frozen VLP models for open-vocabulary multi-label classification. The proposed method consists of two components: a visual token extraction module that derives localized visual tokens from spatial features, and a feature space sharing mechanism that assigns tokens to labels through similarity maximization. By decoupling token extraction from label matching and avoiding knowledge distillation, the framework maintains a simple training pipeline while enabling reusable visual representations across labels. Experiments on NUS-WIDE and MS-COCO under both Zero-Shot Learning(ZSL) and Generalized Zero-Shot Learning(GZSL) settings demonstrate that VPFSS achieves consistent improvements over representative baselines. The results indicate that explicit modeling of localized tokens combined with cross-label feature sharing provides a practical and effective solution for open-vocabulary multi-label recognition. Published: 2026-03-01T22:49:19+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.784 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zhanfang Zhao; Xiaohan Yang

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113393

Relevance: 0.784 (must_read)

In real-world image understanding, images often contain multiple semantic concepts, including rare or previously unseen categories, posing significant challenges for multi-label classification. Multi-Label Zero-Shot Learning (ML-ZSL) aims to recognize unseen labels without requiring annotated training data. While recent approaches based on Vision-Language Pre-training (VLP) models have shown promise, they often struggle to preserve fine-grained local visual features that are critical for multi-label recognition. In this work, we present Visual Projector with Feature Space Sharing (VPFSS), a lightweight framework that adapts frozen VLP models for open-vocabulary multi-label classification. The proposed method consists of two components: a visual token extraction module that derives localized visual tokens from spatial features, and a feature space sharing mechanism that assigns tokens to labels through similarity maximization. By decoupling token extraction from label matching and avoiding knowledge distillation, the framework maintains a simple training pipeline while enabling reusable visual representations across labels. Experiments on NUS-WIDE and MS-COCO under both Zero-Shot Learning(ZSL) and Generalized Zero-Shot Learning(GZSL) settings demonstrate that VPFSS achieves consistent improvements over representative baselines. The results indicate that explicit modeling of localized tokens combined with cross-label feature sharing provides a practical and effective solution for open-vocabulary multi-label recognition.

item.title: CGSA: Class-Guided Slot-Aware Adaptation for Source-Free Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Boyang Dai
Zeng Fan
Zihao Qi
Meng Lou
Yizhou Yu
item.description: Source-Free Domain Adaptive Object Detection (SF-DAOD) aims to adapt a detector trained on a labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain without retaining any source data. Despite recent progress, most popular approaches focus on tuning pseudo-label thresholds or refining the teacher-student framework, while overlooking object-level structural cues within cross-domain data. In this work, we present CGSA, the first framework that brings Object-Centric Learning (OCL) into SF-DAOD by integrating slot-aware adaptation into the DETR-based detector. Specifically, our approach integrates a Hierarchical Slot Awareness (HSA) module into the detector to progressively disentangle images into slot representations that act as visual priors. These slots are then guided toward class semantics via a Class-Guided Slot Contrast (CGSC) module, maintaining semantic consistency and prompting domain-invariant adaptation. Extensive experiments on multiple cross-domain datasets demonstrate that our approach outperforms previous SF-DAOD methods, with theoretical derivations and experimental analysis further demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed components and the framework, thereby indicating the promise of object-centric design in privacy-sensitive adaptation scenarios. Code is released at https://github.com/Michael-McQueen/CGSA. Published: 2026-02-26T04:54:39+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.783 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Boyang Dai; Zeng Fan; Zihao Qi; Meng Lou; Yizhou Yu

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.783 (must_read)

Source-Free Domain Adaptive Object Detection (SF-DAOD) aims to adapt a detector trained on a labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain without retaining any source data. Despite recent progress, most popular approaches focus on tuning pseudo-label thresholds or refining the teacher-student framework, while overlooking object-level structural cues within cross-domain data. In this work, we present CGSA, the first framework that brings Object-Centric Learning (OCL) into SF-DAOD by integrating slot-aware adaptation into the DETR-based detector. Specifically, our approach integrates a Hierarchical Slot Awareness (HSA) module into the detector to progressively disentangle images into slot representations that act as visual priors. These slots are then guided toward class semantics via a Class-Guided Slot Contrast (CGSC) module, maintaining semantic consistency and prompting domain-invariant adaptation. Extensive experiments on multiple cross-domain datasets demonstrate that our approach outperforms previous SF-DAOD methods, with theoretical derivations and experimental analysis further demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed components and the framework, thereby indicating the promise of object-centric design in privacy-sensitive adaptation scenarios. Code is released at https://github.com/Michael-McQueen/CGSA.

item.title: Compress the Easy, Explore the Hard: Difficulty-Aware Entropy Regularization for Efficient LLM Reasoning
item.dc:creator: Qin-Wen Luo
Sheng Ren
Xiang Chen
Rui Liu
Jun Fang
Naiqiang Tan
Sheng-Jun Huang
item.description: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) has substantially empowered Large Language Models (LLMs) to tackle complex reasoning tasks, yet the verbose nature of explicit reasoning steps incurs prohibitive inference latency and computational costs, limiting real-world deployment. While existing compression methods - ranging from self-training to Reinforcement Learning (RL) with length constraints - attempt to mitigate this, they often sacrifice reasoning capability for brevity. We identify a critical failure mode in these approaches: explicitly optimizing for shorter trajectories triggers rapid entropy collapse, which prematurely shrinks the exploration space and stifles the discovery of valid reasoning paths, particularly for challenging questions requiring extensive deduction. To address this issue, we propose Compress responses for Easy questions and Explore Hard ones (CEEH), a difficulty-aware approach to RL-based efficient reasoning. CEEH dynamically assesses instance difficulty to apply selective entropy regularization: it preserves a diverse search space for currently hard questions to ensure robustness, while permitting aggressive compression on easier instances where the reasoning path is well-established. In addition, we introduce a dynamic optimal-length penalty anchored to the historically shortest correct response, which effectively counteracts entropy-induced length inflation and stabilizes the reward signal. Across six reasoning benchmarks, CEEH consistently reduces response length while maintaining accuracy comparable to the base model, and improves Pass@k relative to length-only optimization. Published: 2026-02-26T05:47:30+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.783 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Qin-Wen Luo; Sheng Ren; Xiang Chen; Rui Liu; Jun Fang; Naiqiang Tan; Sheng-Jun Huang

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.783 (must_read)

Chain-of-Thought (CoT) has substantially empowered Large Language Models (LLMs) to tackle complex reasoning tasks, yet the verbose nature of explicit reasoning steps incurs prohibitive inference latency and computational costs, limiting real-world deployment. While existing compression methods - ranging from self-training to Reinforcement Learning (RL) with length constraints - attempt to mitigate this, they often sacrifice reasoning capability for brevity. We identify a critical failure mode in these approaches: explicitly optimizing for shorter trajectories triggers rapid entropy collapse, which prematurely shrinks the exploration space and stifles the discovery of valid reasoning paths, particularly for challenging questions requiring extensive deduction. To address this issue, we propose Compress responses for Easy questions and Explore Hard ones (CEEH), a difficulty-aware approach to RL-based efficient reasoning. CEEH dynamically assesses instance difficulty to apply selective entropy regularization: it preserves a diverse search space for currently hard questions to ensure robustness, while permitting aggressive compression on easier instances where the reasoning path is well-established. In addition, we introduce a dynamic optimal-length penalty anchored to the historically shortest correct response, which effectively counteracts entropy-induced length inflation and stabilizes the reward signal. Across six reasoning benchmarks, CEEH consistently reduces response length while maintaining accuracy comparable to the base model, and improves Pass@k relative to length-only optimization.

item.title: Interactive Edge Awareness Network for Salient Object Detection in Optical Remote Sensing Images
item.dc:creator: Xian Fang
Qihang Wang
Qiaohong Chen
Gongyang Li
item.description: Salient object detection in optical remote sensing images (ORSI-SOD) has gained significant attention due to its wide range of applications. However, the complex scenes and large scale variations in ORSI still pose persistent challenges, including deficient boundary representation, severe background clutter, and semantic degradation. Moreover, most existing methods tend to address these challenges independently, lacking an effective mechanism to collaboratively integrate complementary cues. To bridge this gap, we propose the interactive edge awareness network (IEANet) for ORSI-SOD. Specifically, the Interactive Edge Awareness Module (IEAM) integrates a three-branch feature interaction mechanism with an average-max pooling difference strategy, effectively capturing boundary information and adaptively enhancing edge representations without explicit edge supervision. To suppress background clutter and boost multiscale object perception, context attention guidance module (CAGM) employs dual-path attention to highlight salient regions through global-local feature fusion. Moreover, semantic consistency preservation module (SCPM) adopts an attention-driven strategy that alleviates semantic degradation during upsampling and further facilitates the discriminative power of feature decoding in complex environments. Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of IEANet, which achieves superior performance over 22 state-of-the-art methods on most of the eight evaluation metrics, with the compared methods reporting average S-measure, mean E-measure, and MAE values of 92.53%, 96.11%, and 0.69%, respectively. Published: 2026-03-01T06:50:47+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.781 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xian Fang; Qihang Wang; Qiaohong Chen; Gongyang Li

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.131879

Relevance: 0.781 (must_read)

Salient object detection in optical remote sensing images (ORSI-SOD) has gained significant attention due to its wide range of applications. However, the complex scenes and large scale variations in ORSI still pose persistent challenges, including deficient boundary representation, severe background clutter, and semantic degradation. Moreover, most existing methods tend to address these challenges independently, lacking an effective mechanism to collaboratively integrate complementary cues. To bridge this gap, we propose the interactive edge awareness network (IEANet) for ORSI-SOD. Specifically, the Interactive Edge Awareness Module (IEAM) integrates a three-branch feature interaction mechanism with an average-max pooling difference strategy, effectively capturing boundary information and adaptively enhancing edge representations without explicit edge supervision. To suppress background clutter and boost multiscale object perception, context attention guidance module (CAGM) employs dual-path attention to highlight salient regions through global-local feature fusion. Moreover, semantic consistency preservation module (SCPM) adopts an attention-driven strategy that alleviates semantic degradation during upsampling and further facilitates the discriminative power of feature decoding in complex environments. Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of IEANet, which achieves superior performance over 22 state-of-the-art methods on most of the eight evaluation metrics, with the compared methods reporting average S-measure, mean E-measure, and MAE values of 92.53%, 96.11%, and 0.69%, respectively.

item.title: SideQuest: Model-Driven KV Cache Management for Long-Horizon Agentic Reasoning
item.dc:creator: Sanjay Kariyappa
G. Edward Suh
item.description: Long-running agentic tasks, such as deep research, require multi-hop reasoning over information distributed across multiple webpages and documents. In such tasks, the LLM context is dominated by tokens from external retrieval, causing memory usage to grow rapidly and limiting decode performance. While several KV cache compression techniques exist for long-context inputs, we find that existing heuristics fail to support multi-step reasoning models effectively. We address this challenge with SideQuest -- a novel approach that leverages the Large Reasoning Model (LRM) itself to perform KV cache compression by reasoning about the usefulness of tokens in its context. To prevent the tokens associated with this management process from polluting the model's memory, we frame KV cache compression as an auxiliary task executed in parallel to the main reasoning task. Our evaluations, using a model trained with just 215 samples, show that SideQuest reduces peak token usage by up to 65% on agentic tasks with minimal degradation in accuracy, outperforming heuristic-based KV cache compression techniques. Published: 2026-02-26T04:20:44+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.780 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Sanjay Kariyappa; G. Edward Suh

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.780 (must_read)

Long-running agentic tasks, such as deep research, require multi-hop reasoning over information distributed across multiple webpages and documents. In such tasks, the LLM context is dominated by tokens from external retrieval, causing memory usage to grow rapidly and limiting decode performance. While several KV cache compression techniques exist for long-context inputs, we find that existing heuristics fail to support multi-step reasoning models effectively. We address this challenge with SideQuest -- a novel approach that leverages the Large Reasoning Model (LRM) itself to perform KV cache compression by reasoning about the usefulness of tokens in its context. To prevent the tokens associated with this management process from polluting the model's memory, we frame KV cache compression as an auxiliary task executed in parallel to the main reasoning task. Our evaluations, using a model trained with just 215 samples, show that SideQuest reduces peak token usage by up to 65% on agentic tasks with minimal degradation in accuracy, outperforming heuristic-based KV cache compression techniques.

item.title: Fourier Angle Alignment for Oriented Object Detection in Remote Sensing
item.dc:creator: Changyu Gu
Linwei Chen
Lin Gu
Ying Fu
item.description: In remote sensing rotated object detection, mainstream methods suffer from two bottlenecks, directional incoherence at detector neck and task conflict at detecting head. Ulitising fourier rotation equivariance, we introduce Fourier Angle Alignment, which analyses angle information through frequency spectrum and aligns the main direction to a certain orientation. Then we propose two plug and play modules : FAAFusion and FAA Head. FAAFusion works at the detector neck, aligning the main direction of higher-level features to the lower-level features and then fusing them. FAA Head serves as a new detection head, which pre-aligns RoI features to a canonical angle and adds them to the original features before classification and regression. Experiments on DOTA-v1.0, DOTA-v1.5 and HRSC2016 show that our method can greatly improve previous work. Particularly, our method achieves new state-of-the-art results of 78.72% mAP on DOTA-v1.0 and 72.28% mAP on DOTA-v1.5 datasets with single scale training and testing, validating the efficacy of our approach in remote sensing object detection. The code is made publicly available at https://github.com/gcy0423/Fourier-Angle-Alignment . Published: 2026-02-27T08:34:57+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.779 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Changyu Gu; Linwei Chen; Lin Gu; Ying Fu

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

In remote sensing rotated object detection, mainstream methods suffer from two bottlenecks, directional incoherence at detector neck and task conflict at detecting head. Ulitising fourier rotation equivariance, we introduce Fourier Angle Alignment, which analyses angle information through frequency spectrum and aligns the main direction to a certain orientation. Then we propose two plug and play modules : FAAFusion and FAA Head. FAAFusion works at the detector neck, aligning the main direction of higher-level features to the lower-level features and then fusing them. FAA Head serves as a new detection head, which pre-aligns RoI features to a canonical angle and adds them to the original features before classification and regression. Experiments on DOTA-v1.0, DOTA-v1.5 and HRSC2016 show that our method can greatly improve previous work. Particularly, our method achieves new state-of-the-art results of 78.72% mAP on DOTA-v1.0 and 72.28% mAP on DOTA-v1.5 datasets with single scale training and testing, validating the efficacy of our approach in remote sensing object detection. The code is made publicly available at https://github.com/gcy0423/Fourier-Angle-Alignment .

item.title: SPMamba-YOLO: An Underwater Object Detection Network Based on Multi-Scale Feature Enhancement and Global Context Modeling
item.dc:creator: Guanghao Liao
Zhen Liu
Liyuan Cao
Yonghui Yang
Qi Li
item.description: Underwater object detection is a critical yet challenging research problem owing to severe light attenuation, color distortion, background clutter, and the small scale of underwater targets. To address these challenges, we propose SPMamba-YOLO, a novel underwater object detection network that integrates multi-scale feature enhancement with global context modeling. Specifically, a Spatial Pyramid Pooling Enhanced Layer Aggregation Network (SPPELAN) module is introduced to strengthen multi-scale feature aggregation and expand the receptive field, while a Pyramid Split Attention (PSA) mechanism enhances feature discrimination by emphasizing informative regions and suppressing background interference. In addition, a Mamba-based state space modeling module is incorporated to efficiently capture long-range dependencies and global contextual information, thereby improving detection robustness in complex underwater environments. Extensive experiments on the URPC2022 dataset demonstrate that SPMamba-YOLO outperforms the YOLOv8n baseline by more than 4.9\% in mAP@0.5, particularly for small and densely distributed underwater objects, while maintaining a favorable balance between detection accuracy and computational cost. Published: 2026-02-26T06:45:11+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.779 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Guanghao Liao; Zhen Liu; Liyuan Cao; Yonghui Yang; Qi Li

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.779 (must_read)

Underwater object detection is a critical yet challenging research problem owing to severe light attenuation, color distortion, background clutter, and the small scale of underwater targets. To address these challenges, we propose SPMamba-YOLO, a novel underwater object detection network that integrates multi-scale feature enhancement with global context modeling. Specifically, a Spatial Pyramid Pooling Enhanced Layer Aggregation Network (SPPELAN) module is introduced to strengthen multi-scale feature aggregation and expand the receptive field, while a Pyramid Split Attention (PSA) mechanism enhances feature discrimination by emphasizing informative regions and suppressing background interference. In addition, a Mamba-based state space modeling module is incorporated to efficiently capture long-range dependencies and global contextual information, thereby improving detection robustness in complex underwater environments. Extensive experiments on the URPC2022 dataset demonstrate that SPMamba-YOLO outperforms the YOLOv8n baseline by more than 4.9\% in mAP@0.5, particularly for small and densely distributed underwater objects, while maintaining a favorable balance between detection accuracy and computational cost.

item.title: Spectrally Distilled Representations Aligned with Instruction-Augmented LLMs for Satellite Imagery
item.dc:creator: Minh Kha Do
Wei Xiang
Kang Han
Di Wu
Khoa Phan
Yi-Ping Phoebe Chen
Gaowen Liu
Ramana Rao Kompella
item.description: Vision-language foundation models (VLFMs) promise zero-shot and retrieval understanding for Earth observation. While operational satellite systems often lack full multi-spectral coverage, making RGB-only inference highly desirable for scalable deployment, the adoption of VLFMs for satellite imagery remains hindered by two factors: (1) multi-spectral inputs are informative but difficult to exploit consistently due to band redundancy and misalignment; and (2) CLIP-style text encoders limit semantic expressiveness and weaken fine-grained alignment. We present SATtxt, a spectrum-aware VLFM that operates with RGB inputs only at inference while retaining spectral cues learned during training. Our framework comprises two stages. First, Spectral Representation Distillation transfers spectral priors from a frozen multi-spectral teacher to an RGB student via a lightweight projector. Second, Spectrally Grounded Alignment with Instruction-Augmented LLMs bridges the distilled visual space and an expressive LLM embedding space. Across EuroSAT, BigEarthNet, and ForestNet, SATtxt improves zero-shot classification on average by 4.2%, retrieval by 5.9%, and linear probing by 2.7% over baselines, showing an efficient path toward spectrum-aware vision-language learning for Earth observation. Project page: https://ikhado.github.io/sattxt/ Published: 2026-02-26T04:34:47+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.778 (must_read)

Authors: Minh Kha Do; Wei Xiang; Kang Han; Di Wu; Khoa Phan; Yi-Ping Phoebe Chen; Gaowen Liu; Ramana Rao Kompella

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.778 (must_read)

Vision-language foundation models (VLFMs) promise zero-shot and retrieval understanding for Earth observation. While operational satellite systems often lack full multi-spectral coverage, making RGB-only inference highly desirable for scalable deployment, the adoption of VLFMs for satellite imagery remains hindered by two factors: (1) multi-spectral inputs are informative but difficult to exploit consistently due to band redundancy and misalignment; and (2) CLIP-style text encoders limit semantic expressiveness and weaken fine-grained alignment. We present SATtxt, a spectrum-aware VLFM that operates with RGB inputs only at inference while retaining spectral cues learned during training. Our framework comprises two stages. First, Spectral Representation Distillation transfers spectral priors from a frozen multi-spectral teacher to an RGB student via a lightweight projector. Second, Spectrally Grounded Alignment with Instruction-Augmented LLMs bridges the distilled visual space and an expressive LLM embedding space. Across EuroSAT, BigEarthNet, and ForestNet, SATtxt improves zero-shot classification on average by 4.2%, retrieval by 5.9%, and linear probing by 2.7% over baselines, showing an efficient path toward spectrum-aware vision-language learning for Earth observation. Project page: https://ikhado.github.io/sattxt/

item.title: Decoupling time-invariant and time-variant features for sensor fusion in target classification
item.dc:creator: Yida Wu
Caiyun Wang
Jianing Wang
Xiaofei Li
Ying Nan
item.description: In sensor fusion classification tasks, an essential challenge lies in how to effectively integrate heterogeneous information from multiple sensors to achieve accurate and robust classification. Existing fusion strategies can be broadly categorized into concatenation-based, reconstruction-based, and modality decoupling methods. However, these approaches often lack interpretability of the fused features and may fail to fully exploit and leverage the complementary and consistent characteristics among sensors. To overcome these limitations, we propose a novel framework that explicitly decouples time-invariant (TI) and time-variant (TV) features, which respectively represent the static intrinsic attributes and the dynamic temporal variations of the target. Our framework employs a time disentanglement (TD) module that individually decouples TI and TV features for each sensor. For the TI features, we design a modality disentanglement (MD) module to fuse complementary information by separating modality-shared and modality-specific representations. For the TV features, we introduce a cross attention (CA) module to improve the consistency of dynamic representations across sensors by capturing their temporal correlations. Experiments were conducted on Radar-Infrared fusion recognition and human activity recognition (HAR) tasks to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed framework. The results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly improves recognition accuracy and robustness compared to conventional fusion methods, validating its ability to integrate heterogeneous sensor data and jointly model both static and dynamic target characteristics. Published: 2026-02-28T23:09:16+00:00 Venue: Neurocomputing Score: 0.777 (must_read)
